source_paper_title: A Primal-Dual Smoothing Framework for Max-Structured Non-Convex Optimization
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Zhao_2024, title={A Primal-Dual Smoothing Framework for Max-Structured Non-Convex Optimization}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1387}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1387}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Zhao, Renbo}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1535–1565} }
context:
Section 8 (Conclusion and future work), page 35.

First, the lower complexity bound for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-NS point of (1.1) is not known yet. Establishing this bound with dependence on the problem parameters (including \(L_{xx}, L_{xy}, L_{yy}\) and \(\gamma\)) and the accuracy \(\varepsilon\) can be useful to understand the optimality of the existing methods (including ours).
title: Lower bound on oracle complexity for near-stationary max-structured nonconvex optimization
background_latex:
Let \((X,\|\cdot\|_X)\) and \((Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)\) be finite-dimensional real normed spaces. Let \(r:X\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) be closed and convex with domain \(\mathrm{dom}r\subseteq X\), and write \(\mathcal{X}:=\mathrm{dom}r\) (assumed nonempty, closed, convex). Let \(\mathcal{Y}\subseteq Y\) be nonempty, closed, convex, and bounded. Let \(g:\mathcal{Y}\to\mathbb{R}\) be convex and continuous.

Let \(\Phi:\mathcal{X}'\times\mathcal{Y}'\to\mathbb{R}\) be jointly continuous on open sets \(\mathcal{X}'\supseteq \mathcal{X}\), \(\mathcal{Y}'\supseteq \mathcal{Y}\), with \(\Phi(x,\cdot)\) concave on \(\mathcal{Y}\) for each \(x\in\mathcal{X}\). Assume \(\Phi(\cdot,y)\) is Frchet differentiable and has Lipschitz gradient in \(x\): there is \(L_{xx}<\infty\) such that for all \(x,x'\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\|\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_x\Phi(x',y)\|_{X^*}\le L_{xx}\|x-x'\|_X.\]
Assume also a cross-Lipschitz bound: there is \(L_{xy}<\infty\) such that for all \(x\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y,y'\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\|\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_x\Phi(x,y')\|_{X^*}\le L_{xy}\|y-y'\|_Y.\]
Assume weak convexity in \(x\): there exists \(\gamma\in(0,L_{xx}]\) such that for all \(x,x'\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\Phi(x',y)\ge \Phi(x,y)+\langle \nabla_x\Phi(x,y),x'-x\rangle-\tfrac{\gamma}{2}\|x'-x\|_X^2.\]
Assume \(\Phi(x,\cdot)\) is Frchet differentiable with Lipschitz gradient in \(y\): there exist \(L_{yy}<\infty\) and the same \(L_{xy}\) such that for all \(x,x'\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y,y'\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\|\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_y\Phi(x',y)\|_{Y^*}\le L_{xy}\|x-x'\|_X,\qquad \|\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_y\Phi(x,y')\|_{Y^*}\le L_{yy}\|y-y'\|_Y.\]

Define the max-structured nonconvex objective
\[q(x):=f(x)+r(x),\qquad f(x):=\max_{y\in\mathcal{Y}}\ \Phi(x,y)-g(y),\qquad q^*:=\inf_{x\in\mathcal{X}} q(x)>-\infty.\]

Fix a distance generating function (DGF) \(\omega_X:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}\) that is 1-strongly convex on \(\mathcal{X}\) and differentiable on a neighborhood of \(\mathcal{X}\), and assume \(\nabla \omega_X\) is \(\beta_X\)-Lipschitz on \(\mathcal{X}\) for some \(\beta_X\ge1\). Let the induced Bregman divergence be
\[D_{\omega_X}(x',x):=\omega_X(x')-\omega_X(x)-\langle \nabla\omega_X(x),x'-x\rangle.\]
For any \(\lambda\in(0,\gamma^{-1})\), define the proximal point mapping
\[\mathrm{prox}(q,x,\lambda):=\arg\min_{x'\in\mathcal{X}}\ q(x')+\lambda^{-1}D_{\omega_X}(x',x).\]
An \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point is a point \(x\in\mathcal{X}\) for which there exists \(\lambda\in(0,\gamma^{-1})\) such that
\[\|\lambda^{-1}(x-\mathrm{prox}(q,x,\lambda))\|_X\le \varepsilon/\beta_X.\]

The paper studies first-order methods whose cost is measured by the number of evaluations of \(\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)\) and \(\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)\) (and associated simple Bregman proximal projections for \(r\) and \(g\)), yielding upper bounds on the oracle complexities to obtain an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point. The open question concerns matching information-theoretic (worst-case) lower bounds as functions of \(\varepsilon\) and the parameters \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine a worst-case lower bound (in terms of \(\varepsilon\) and the problem parameters \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\), and any necessary dependence on \(\beta_X\)) on the number of first-order oracle calls required by any algorithm to output an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of
\[\min_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\ q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal{Y}}\ \Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x),\]
over the class of instances satisfying the smoothness, cross-Lipschitz, and weak-convexity conditions stated in the background. Here a first-order oracle call reveals \(\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)\) and/or \(\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)\) at queried \((x,y)\in\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y}\), along with the ability to compute the required Bregman proximal projections for \(r\) and \(g\).
latex:

Determine a worst-case lower bound (in terms of \(\varepsilon\) and the problem parameters \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\), and any necessary dependence on \(\beta_X\)) on the number of first-order oracle calls required by any algorithm to output an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of
\[\min_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\ q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal{Y}}\ \Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x),\]
over the class of instances satisfying the smoothness, cross-Lipschitz, and weak-convexity conditions stated in the background. Here a first-order oracle call reveals \(\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)\) and/or \(\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)\) at queried \((x,y)\in\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y}\), along with the ability to compute the required Bregman proximal projections for \(r\) and \(g\).
open_problem_quote: First, the lower complexity bound for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-NS point of (1.1) is not known yet. Establishing this bound with dependence on the problem parameters (including \(L_{xx}, L_{xy}, L_{yy}\) and \(\gamma\)) and the accuracy \(\varepsilon\) can be useful to understand the optimality of the existing methods (including ours).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Lower bounds typically require adversarial instance constructions and information-based arguments rather than searching for a finite counterexample. While one can numerically test hard instances, that does not certify a universal oracle lower bound.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary code loop could search for hard instance families (specific \(\Phi,g,r\), norms, and DGFs) that empirically maximize the number of oracle calls needed by candidate algorithms, providing conjectures and candidate worst-case constructions. However, the fitness signal (measured complexity) is noisy and algorithm-dependent, and translating discovered instances into rigorous lower-bound proofs remains nontrivial.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for information-theoretic lower bounds (in terms of \(\varepsilon\) and parameters \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\), and possibly \(\beta_X\)) on the number of first-order oracle calls needed to output an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point for the max-structured weakly convex objective \(q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal Y}\Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\), where the oracle reveals \(\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)\) and/or \(\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)\). Zhao’s paper provides upper bounds via a primal-dual smoothing framework: in the Hilbertian baseline (and more generally with Bregman geometry), the deterministic complexities scale essentially as \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) (Corollary 4.2), with explicit dependence on \(\gamma\) and the smoothness/cross-Lipschitz constants through the cost of solving strongly convex-concave saddle-point subproblems.

At present, there is no matching lower bound specialized to this oracle model and problem class in the provided materials, and Zhao explicitly lists this as open in Section 8. Existing foundational tools (Danskin-type subdifferential calculus for max functions, and Nesterov-style dual smoothing) explain why the algorithmic upper bounds naturally involve quantities like \(L_{xx}+L_{xy}^2/\rho\) after smoothing and why inner saddle-point solvers (e.g., Mirror-Prox-type methods) introduce dependence on \(L_{yy}\) and \(L_{xy}\). However, these works do not establish worst-case lower bounds for producing \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationarity in the max-structured nonconvex setting.

Promising directions for lower bounds include: (i) reductions from known lower bounds for finding \(\varepsilon\)-stationary points of \(\gamma\)-weakly convex functions under first-order oracles, adapted to the structured oracle that queries \((x,y)\) and returns \(\nabla_x\Phi,\nabla_y\Phi\); (ii) embedding hard instances of smooth nonconvex minimization or smooth nonconvex-concave minimax (as in the smooth minimax literature) into the max-structured template by choosing \(\mathcal Y\) and \(g\) so that \(f(x)=\max_y \Phi(x,y)-g(y)\) reproduces the hard function while controlling \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy}\); and (iii) separating regimes where \(\gamma\ll L_{xx}\) to test whether the improved \(\sqrt{\gamma L_{xx}}\) dependence in Zhao’s upper bound can be shown necessary. Establishing such lower bounds would clarify whether \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) is optimal and how the cross-Lipschitz constants fundamentally limit progress per oracle call.

sources.title: The theory of Max-Min and its application to weapons allocation problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides Danskin-type results characterizing directional derivatives/subgradients of max-functions, underpinning formulas like \(f'(x;d)=\sup_{y\in Y^*(x)}\langle \nabla_x\Phi(x,y),d\rangle\).
sources.connection: Lower-bound constructions for max-structured objectives often exploit the max representation and its subdifferential structure; Danskin’s theorem is the basic tool used in the source paper (Lemma 3.1) and in many complexity lower bounds for nonsmooth/nonconvex max objectives.

sources.title: Smooth minimization of non-smooth functions
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces smoothing of max-structured convex functions via strongly convex regularization in the dual, yielding smooth approximations with Lipschitz gradients scaling like \(L_{xx}+L_{xy}^2/\rho\).
sources.connection: Zhao’s framework relies on dual smoothing \(f_\rho\) and the induced smoothness \(L_\rho=L_{xx}+L_{xy}^2/\rho\) (Lemma 3.5). Lower bounds may need to account for the necessity of such smoothing and the \(L_{xy}^2/\rho\) dependence when only \(\nabla_x\Phi,\nabla_y\Phi\) are accessible.

sources.title: Prox-method with rate of convergence O(1/t) for variational inequalities with lipschitz continuous monotone operators and smooth convex-concave saddle point problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops Mirror-Prox for monotone variational inequalities / smooth convex-concave saddle-point problems with \(O(1/t)\) gap rate under Lipschitz operator assumptions.
sources.connection: Zhao’s Step 2 subproblem is a strongly convex-concave saddle-point problem; many upper bounds (and potential lower bounds) for such oracle models are benchmarked against Mirror-Prox-type rates, which influence the achievable dependence on \(L_{yy},L_{xy}\) in inner solves.

sources.title: Stochastic model-based minimization of weakly convex functions
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops model-based/proximal methods for \(\gamma\)-weakly convex minimization targeting near-stationarity via proximal-point envelopes; provides oracle complexity bounds in stochastic settings.
sources.connection: The near-stationarity notion in Zhao (via \(\|\lambda^{-1}(x-\mathrm{prox}(q,x,\lambda))\|\)) is standard in weakly convex optimization. Known lower bounds for weakly convex nonsmooth problems (in other oracle models) may be adaptable to the max-structured oracle model to prove \(\varepsilon\)-dependence lower bounds.

sources.title: Proximally guided stochastic subgradient method for nonsmooth, nonconvex problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes proximally guided schemes for nonsmooth nonconvex optimization, producing near-stationary points with complexity guarantees under stochastic subgradient access.
sources.connection: Provides a baseline for weakly convex near-stationarity complexity and motivates the need for lower bounds. Although Zhao’s oracle is structured (access to \(\nabla_x\Phi,\nabla_y\Phi\)), lower bounds might reduce to these more general nonsmooth models.

sources.title: An accelerated inexact proximal point method for solving nonconvex-concave min-max problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Proposes accelerated inexact proximal-point schemes for nonconvex-concave min-max problems and analyzes complexity for finding near-stationary points of smoothed objectives.
sources.connection: Zhao compares against this line and improves parameter dependence when \(\gamma\ll L_{xx}\). Any lower bound should clarify whether \(\varepsilon^{-3}\) (up to logs) is unavoidable and how \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\) must enter.

sources.title: Efficient algorithms for smooth minimax optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives first-order methods for smooth nonconvex-concave minimax problems with complexity bounds (e.g., \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) for certain stationarity notions).
sources.connection: Zhao’s Table 1 benchmarks against this work in the Hilbertian, \(r\equiv g\equiv 0\) case. Lower bounds for smooth minimax (or reductions from it) are a natural starting point for proving oracle lower bounds in the max-structured model.

sources.title: Non-convex min-max optimization: Provable algorithms and applications in machine learning
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies algorithms and complexity for nonconvex-concave min-max problems, including stochastic settings, often yielding \(\varepsilon\)-stationarity rates like \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-6})\) in stochastic regimes.
sources.connection: Zhao notes the stochastic extension matches \(O(\varepsilon^{-6})\). Lower bounds in stochastic min-max/weakly convex settings may inform what is information-theoretically necessary, and whether deterministic \(\varepsilon^{-3}\) can be improved.
